steps.title: Identify what's being praised
steps.content:
The poem directly addresses Dunbar with admiration, repeatedly praising what he “reads” or perceives.

The speaker emphasizes two distinct domains: “the hearts and souls of men” (human understanding) and “the language of the flowers” and “the little brook” (natural perception).

The framing—”mighty pen,” “Responded to thy call”—reveals the speaker is celebrating these abilities as remarkable.

steps.title: Match the purpose to the choices
steps.content:
Choice A captures exactly what the poem does: it praises Dunbar for being perceptive about people and nature.

The other choices distort the poem's focus—
  • B reinterprets “read” as studying many topics rather than perceiving
  • C invents a detailed writing process the poem never mentions
  • D fabricates a narrative of a shared afternoon that doesn't exist in the text
.

That matches choice A — A is correct.
